It begins with the inflammatory phase, where the body's natural response to injury sets in, and white blood cells, such as neutrophils and macrophages, are activated to clean up debris and bacteria from the affected area. The ancient Egyptians referred to their land as "Kemet," which translates to "Black Land" in English. This name was not given due to the skin color of its inhabitants, but rather because of the rich, dark soil that characterized the Nile River valley. The Nile's annual flooding brought nutrient-rich silt that made the land suitable for agriculture, allowing the Egyptians to cultivate crops and settle in the area. In contrast, the surrounding desert regions were known as "Desheret" or "Red Land," due to their arid and barren conditions.
